attributes.stem: A 58-year-old woman presents with a several-month history of dysphagia, reflux symptoms, and unintentional weight loss. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy reveals a large ulcerated mass located at the gastro-oesophageal junction, with a biopsy confirming adenocarcinoma of the lower oesophagus. Imaging studies indicate no evidence of distant metastases. After multidisciplinary consultation, the patient undergoes a total oesophagectomy with reconstruction by gastric pull-up technique.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following arteries is preserved by the surgeon during this surgery?
attributes.explanation:
This item requires a knowledge of the blood supply to the stomach. During a total oesophagectomy with reconstruction by gastric pull-up technique, it will obviously be essential to ensure that sufficient vascularity to the stomach is maintained. To do this, the surgeon typically preserves the right gastro-epiploic artery. This artery arises from the gastroduodenal artery when it divides into its two terminal branches; the right gastroepiploic artery and the superior pancreaticoduodenal artery. It provides an important blood supply to the gastric tube used for reconstruction. Preserving this artery helps ensure adequate blood flow to the gastric tube, promoting its viability and reducing the risk of complications such as ischaemia at the anastomotic site and leakage.

Total oesophagectomy involves the surgical removal of the entire oesophagus and adjacent lymph nodes, typically performed to treat advanced oesophageal cancer or severe oesophageal dysmotility disorders. Following oesophagectomy, reconstruction is essential to restore gastrointestinal continuity and preserve the swallowing function. Various reconstruction techniques exist, with the gastric pull-up being the most common. In this approach, the stomach is mobilised and brought into the chest or neck to serve as a conduit for replacing the removed oesophagus. Preservation of the right gastroepiploic artery is crucial to ensure adequate blood supply to the gastric conduit. Other reconstruction options include colon interposition and free flap techniques using tissues like the jejunum.

During total oesophagectomy with gastric pull-up reconstruction, the left gastric artery is typically ligated or divided. This artery originates from the coeliac trunk and supplies blood to the lesser curvature of the stomach and the cardiac region. The left gastro-epiploic artery arises from the splenic artery and travels along the greater curvature of the stomach, supplying blood to the greater omentum and fundus. The right gastric artery is also divided during gastric pull-up reconstruction. Arising from the hepatic artery, it supplies blood to the distal lesser curvature of the stomach. The short gastric arteries arise from the splenic artery and supply the fundus and upper part of the greater curvature of the stomach.

During total oesophagectomy with gastric pull-up reconstruction, these arteries are typically ligated or divided, as they do not play a significant role in maintaining blood supply to the gastric conduit.
learningPoints: The gastric pull-up technique, which involves mobilising the stomach and utilising it as a conduit for oesophageal reconstruction following total oesophagectomy, relies on preserving the blood supply primarily from the right gastroepiploic artery to ensure the viability of the gastric conduit.
references: Baldwin D, Gonzalez SR, Anand S. Esophageal Reconstruction. [Updated 2023 Apr 24]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK564336/
